topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice was tacked onto the bulletin board, a stark, beige rectangle against the peeling paint of the hallway. I stared at the words—"Campus Operations Cease on June 30th"—and a cold, hollow sensation settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I didn't move to read the fine print. Instead, I slowly pushed the notice away with my index finger, the sound of the paper tearing a small, sharp sound in the otherwise silent corridor. My hands were trembling slightly, a faint tremor running up my forearms. I looked around the empty lecture hall, at the vacant desks where hundreds of late-night study sessions had once occurred. There was a profound sense of emptiness, a sickening realization that the structure I had poured so much of myself into was simply being dismantled, rendered obsolete. I felt a tightness in my throat, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 2]

"So, this is it?" I asked, the words coming out flat, devoid of any real inflection. My voice sounded thin, almost brittle, as I stood by the window of the library, watching the autumn leaves turn brittle and fall. Every movement felt sluggish, weighted down by an invisible anchor. I picked up a worn textbook, the spine cracking under my grip, and let it slip onto the floor. The sound was too loud in the cavernous space. I didn't bother to pick it up again. My gaze drifted to the administration building across the quad, a monolithic structure that suddenly seemed monstrous, indifferent. A wave of nausea washed over me, a churning sensation in my stomach that made my skin crawl. I wanted to turn and flee, but my feet felt rooted to the spot, unable to summon the energy for escape.

[story 3]

I traced the faded outline of my student ID card with my thumb, the plastic cool and utterly meaningless. The air in the administrative office was stale, thick with the smell of old paper and something vaguely chemical. When the dean finally delivered the news, his tone was measured, professional, utterly devoid of the warmth I had once associated with this place. But I heard nothing of the words; I only registered the way my jaw clenched, the involuntary tightening around my mouth. I leaned back in the uncomfortable chair, the springs groaning in protest beneath me. I looked down at my hands, noticing the slight discoloration on my knuckles, as if I had been holding something toxic. The reality of the closure didn't register as sadness or even shock; it registered as a profound, sickening wrongness, a fundamental betrayal of everything I had believed about this institution.
